comparisons.name: Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Multiple tiers/programs (Nursing Home entitlement, HCBS waivers with caps, Regular ABD); eligibility pathways vary by marital status/SSI; functional NFLOC assessment key; annual limit updates.
gotchas: Multiple programs with varying rules (e.g., waivers have waitlists, Regular Medicaid requires SSI)
Asset transfers penalized (look-back period)
Dual Medicare/Medicaid eligible still qualify but benefits coordinate
NFLOC required for long-term care, not automatic
Income/asset limits change annually; 2026 figures apply
HCBS prefers home over nursing home; limited waiver slots

comparisons.name: Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Tied to Medicaid State Plan (no waitlist, unlike 1915(c) waivers); requires NFLOC; delivered via managed care like STAR+PLUS with regional MCO variations; income via SSI/300% SSI pathways, assets standard Medicaid with home exemptions
gotchas: Not a standalone waiver—requires underlying Medicaid State Plan eligibility (excludes some STAR+PLUS 1115 waiver participants with income >SSI)
Must meet institutional NFLOC, not just disability/dementia diagnosis
Overlaps/confused with STAR+PLUS HCBS (for 65+/21+ disabled) or other waivers like HCS/TxHmL which have waitlists
Home equity limit $730,000 for exemption; estate recovery risk post-death
No services if already in 1115 waiver category without State Plan tie

comparisons.name: STAR+PLUS

diffs.source: https://www.hhs.texas.gov/services/health/medicaid-chip/medicaid-chip-programs/services-people-who-think-or-have-physical-disabilities/starplus-home-community-based-services-hcbs-waiver (inferred from sources; primary via Your Texas Benefits or HHS)

https://www.hhs.texas.gov/services/health/medicaid-chip/medicaid-chip-programs/services-people-who-think-or-have-physical-disabilities/starplus-home-community-based-services-hcbs-waiver (inferred from sources; primary via Your Texas Benefits or HHS)

diffs.found: Managed care Medicaid program including acute medical care, long-term services/supports (e.g., Day Activity & Health Services up to 5 days/week Mon-Fri for physical/mental/medical/social needs; Personal Attendant Services; nursing facility services if applicable like room/board, medical supplies, rehab); dental/vision; NEMT; pharmacy; service coordination; consumer-directed services; extras vary by plan (e.g., Weight Watchers subscription for qualifying BMI/diagnoses, emergency response up to 6 months post-discharge, asthma supplies, IDD camp allowance up to $100, extra rides).[3][5][6]
diffs.source: https://www.hhs.texas.gov/services/health/medicaid-chip/medicaid-chip-programs/services-people-who-think-or-have-physical-disabilities/starplus-home-community-based-services-hcbs-waiver (inferred from sources; primary via Your Texas Benefits or HHS)

novelFields.value: No more than $2,000 in countable assets. Exemptions include primary home if equity interest ≤ $730,000 and applicant lives there or intends to return, spouse living in home, child under 21 or disabled/blind child of any age living in home.[1][4]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Different managed care organizations/providers by region (e.g., Community First, Molina, Superior, Wellpoint); some extras limited to specific counties (e.g., Weight Watchers in Dallas/Hidalgo); varying wait times.[2][3][5]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Very long interest list (years) for HCBS; exceptions exist.[1]
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of income (SSI award letters, pay stubs)
Proof of assets/resources
Proof of residency (Texas ID/utility bills)
Medical records/physician certification for NFLOC
Home equity documentation if applicable
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Managed care waiver program with mandatory MCO enrollment post-eligibility; long interest list gating HCBS access; benefits tiered by HCBS waiver vs. nursing facility; varies by MCO service area and priority (e.g., SSI vs. non-SSI); NFLOC medical criteria central
gotchas: Long interest list wait (years) for HCBS - must join list first, not direct apply
Income limit applies per applicant ($2,901/mo), not household
Must meet NFLOC (nursing home level need) via physician/MCO assessment
Home equity limit $730,000 if intending to return
Managed care plan selection post-eligibility; auto-assigned if no choice
Cannot be in another waiver while enrolled (but can stay on lists)
Some benefits/extras region/plan-specific or require functional need >$0
comparisons.officialSource: https://www.hhs.texas.gov/services/health/medicaid-chip/medicaid-chip-programs/services-people-who-think-or-have-physical-disabilities/starplus-home-community-based-services-hcbs-waiver (inferred from sources; primary via Your Texas Benefits or HHS)

comparisons.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)

diffs.found: Comprehensive, all-inclusive care at adult day health center and home: primary/acute/specialty medical care, prescription drugs, hospital/inpatient care, therapy (PT/OT/ST), home care (personal attendants, homemaker, respite), transportation, social services, adult day health (meals, activities, therapies ~20-30 hours/week typical), dental/vision/hearing aids, palliative care. No copays/deductibles for enrollees; covers all Medicare/Medicaid-covered + extras. Average participant: age 76, multiple chronic conditions.[1][2][4]
diffs.source: https://www.npaonline.org/find-pace (national locator); https://www.texashealthandhumanservices.gov/services/aging (Texas HHSC); provider sites like https://www.bienvivir.org/eligibility

novelFields.value: No asset limits for PACE enrollment. For Medicaid eligibility: $2,000 countable assets for individual (exempt: primary home, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots). Prepaid funeral expenses and other strategies may exempt more via Medicaid planning.[2][6]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Different providers per region (e.g., Bienvivir El Paso requires state app assist; waitlists/capacities vary; services tailored but core same).[5]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Common due to limited centers; lengths vary by region (e.g., months to years; check provider).[1]
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: ID (driver's license, passport), SSN, Medicare/Medicaid cards.
Income proof (SSI award letters, bank statements, pension docs).
Asset statements (bank/brokerage excluding exempt).
Medical records (physician referral, ADL assessments).
Proof of residency in service area, legal status.
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Limited to specific regional providers/centers (not statewide); no direct income/asset test for PACE but tied to Medicaid for free access; dual-eligible focus; service area residency strict; waitlisted/high demand.
gotchas: Not statewide—must live in one of few service areas near a PACE center; no eligibility if outside.
90%+ are dual-eligible; private pay very expensive ($7k+/mo) if not Medicaid-qualified.
Cannot be on Medicare Advantage/hospice; must disenroll first.
Waitlists long in popular areas; nursing home cert required but must prove community safety.
Voluntary but hard to exit if health declines (full risk on PACE).[1][2][4][6]
comparisons.officialSource: https://www.npaonline.org/find-pace (national locator); https://www.texashealthandhumanservices.gov/services/aging (Texas HHSC); provider sites like https://www.bienvivir.org/eligibility

comparisons.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

diffs.found: QMB: Pays Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums (~$185/month 2026), annual deductible, coinsurance/copayments for Medicare-covered Parts A/B services. SLMB: Pays Part B premiums only. QI: Pays Part B premiums only; auto-qualifies for Extra Help (prescription drugs, max $12.65 copay/drug 2026).[1][6]

novelFields.value: 2026 resource limits (countable assets): Individual: $9,950; Married couple: $14,910. Counts: Cash, bank accounts, stocks, bonds (excluding exempt items). Exempt: Primary home, one vehicle, household goods, wedding/engagement rings, burial plots, up to $1,500 burial expenses, life insurance under $1,500 face value, certain Native American funds.[4][6]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered uniformly by HHSC but local offices may have slight processing differences; no major benefit variations[2]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: QI: First-come, first-served; limited federal funding may create waitlist after slots fill (priority to prior year recipients).[1][6]
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of Medicare enrollment (card)
Income: Pay stubs, SSI/SSDI awards, pension statements (2-3 months)
Assets: Bank statements, investment accounts, property deeds
ID: SSN, birth certificate, proof of residency
Household size details
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Three tiers (QMB/SLMB/QI) with escalating income thresholds (100%/120%/135% FPL) and tiered benefits; QI capped funding creates waitlist risk; income after disregards; statewide but local HHSC processing
gotchas: QI has limited enrollment and requires annual reapplication; may run out of funds mid-year
Must have Part A (and B for SLMB/QI); QMB protects from provider billing for copays (providers can't charge you)
Income disregards often overlooked ($20 + earned income deductions can qualify higher gross incomes)
Assets include spouse's even if not applying; Texas follows federal asset test (some states eliminated it)
Auto-enrolls in Extra Help for Rx but notify plans to avoid duplicate premiums
Outdated limits common; always check current FPL-based figures (change April 1)

comparisons.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Administered via 254 county subrecipients with regional variations in providers and waitlists; benefits via CEAP integration; income at 150% FPG, no asset test; seasonal components.
gotchas: Not year-round: heating (fall/winter), cooling (summer), crisis (emergencies only).[1]
Funding limited by federal availability; subject to waitlists.[4]
Household defined broadly (all sharing utility bill, unlike SNAP).[1]
Must contact specific local agency; no central statewide application.[4][6]
Public/subsidized housing eligibility depends on how energy costs are paid.[2][3]

comparisons.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

diffs.found: Energy audit to identify inefficiencies; installation of weatherization materials including caulking, weather-stripping, ceiling/wall/floor insulation, hole patching, duct work, heating/cooling system tune-up/repair/replacement. Ensures energy savings, health/safety improvements, minor repairs. No fixed dollar amount or hours; scope based on audit using DOE-approved measures (10 CFR Part 440 Appendix A)[5]. Available once every 10-15 years[4][7].

novelFields.value: No asset limits mentioned across sources.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Yes—different providers (e.g., Travis County HHS, SPCAA South Plains, CACOST, CCSCT Uvalde counties, Dallas DCHHS, Austin Energy with unique rules like CAP enrollment/80% MFI); income % FPL may differ (150% LIHEAP vs 200% DOE); forms/sites specific; some add home size/value limits[1][2][3][4][6][7].
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Likely due to funding limits; varies by subrecipient and demand, not detailed[1][2].
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Government-issued photo ID, Military ID, or US Passport[1][3].

Proof of ALL household income last 30 days (pay stubs, SS/SSI award letters 2025/current year, TANF/SNAP within 30 days, unemployment, pension, etc.; no bank statements; notarized declaration if no/unemployed/cash)[1][2][3][6].
SS card/SSN for household members[1].
Proof of citizenship/residency for all (birth cert + ID, passport, etc.)[3].
Utility bills (electric/gas/propane, 12-month history if available)[6].
Household info (names, DOBs)[6].
Landlord agreement if renting[1][4][6].
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Decentralized by 50+ subrecipients per county/service area with unique forms/sites/phone/docs; dual funding (DOE/LIHEAP) creates 150%/200% FPL split; heavy doc burden for 30-day income proof; no central app/age cutoff but elderly prioritized; Austin Energy variant adds MFI/CAP rules.
gotchas: Not direct to TDHCA—must contact county-specific subrecipient from list; availability limited by funding[5].
Two tracks (DOE 200% FPL vs LIHEAP 150% FPL)—check which your area uses; LIHEAP qualifiers often priority for DOE[3].
Income proof strict: 30 days prior, annualized, all adult members, specific docs only (no bank statements)[2][3][6].
Home must qualify (age, size, value, prior participation); renters need landlord sign-off[4].
Elderly priority implied but not guaranteed—competes with disabled/children households[5].
Once every 10-15 years only[4][7].

comparisons.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income/asset test for counseling access; statewide via regional local partners; service-based not financial aid; open to Medicare families/caregivers[1][2][3][8]
gotchas: Not a healthcare or financial benefit provider—only free counseling/advocacy; people mistake it for direct aid[1][4]
Services are free but appointment-based in some areas; contact early (e.g., 3 months before Medicare eligibility)[1]
Assists with low-income programs but eligibility for those separate (e.g., MSP/LIS have income/asset tests)[2]
Operated as HICAP in Texas—use state-specific contact[8]

comparisons.name: Meals on Wheels

diffs.found: One hot, nutritious, often medically-tailored meal delivered weekdays; optional weekend frozen meals; case management/assessment; personal volunteer contact. No fixed dollar amount or hours specified[2].

novelFields.value: No asset limits mentioned across programs.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Operated by independent local providers with varying exact criteria, delivery zones, phone numbers, and short-term options; find via local Area Agency on Aging[1][7].
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Possible in some programs if high demand[1].
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of age/residence[1]
Medical info, dietary restrictions, doctor's name/phone, emergency contacts, health/mobility details[1][4]
Date of birth, SSN (optional), financial docs sometimes but not for eligibility[1][4]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Decentralized local providers per county/region with no uniform state eligibility/income test; service area residency critical; no central Texas application portal.
gotchas: Not statewide; must confirm local provider and exact service area—outside zones ineligible[1][7]
Homebound required; car ownership or easy mobility may disqualify[1]
Assessment/home visit mandatory, not instant signup[1][2][4]
Voluntary contributions expected but no one denied for inability to pay[3][4]
Priority tiers may delay service for non-highest need[5]
Short-term only for some (e.g., 6 weeks recuperation)[3]

comparisons.name: Community Caregiver Support Program
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Sparse data; appears tied to Texas DFPS caregiver regulations (§700.1017 cross-ref §700.1003), not elderly-focused like query assumes; no dedicated elderly caregiver program clearly identified
gotchas: Limited details available; may relate to DFPS child caregiver support rather than elderly (no elderly-specific matches)[5]
Confused with CCAD, CAS, or VA PCAFC which have distinct eligibility/services[1][2][3]
Search results lack comprehensive program details

comparisons.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

novelFields.value: No asset limits; no financial eligibility requirements.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered locally by different agencies (e.g., Cizik School of Nursing in Harris County, The Senior Source in Dallas County, Texoma Council of Governments in Texoma Region, CAPCOG in its area); volunteer availability and specific contacts vary by region.
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

value: No documents needed to contact or receive services; provide details of concerns or facility name when calling.
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income or asset test; advocacy-only for LTC facility residents statewide via local independent programs; volunteer-driven with regional administration
gotchas: Not a direct service provider (e.g., no healthcare or financial aid)—only advocacy and complaint resolution.
Confidential but independent of facilities; ombudsmen cannot determine Medicaid eligibility or conduct PASRR screenings.
Volunteers must be 18+, pass background checks, and have no conflicts of interest (e.g., no facility employment).
Separate ombudsman for state supported living centers or non-LTC issues.
Families may confuse with paid care services; this is free advocacy only.
